meta.title: Competency Map
meta.description: The 4D competency framework for Make Platform PM fluency — how Architecture Judgment, Abstraction Design, Failure Reasoning, and Systems Sequencing build across levels.

blocks.content:
## The 4D Competency Framework

Platform PM fluency is not a single skill — it is a combination of four distinct competencies that must develop in parallel. These are not engineering skills. They are product manager skills applied to deeply technical domains.

## The Four Competencies

### Architecture Judgment

**Evaluating system design tradeoffs with enough depth to challenge, validate, or redirect engineering proposals.**

A PM with strong architecture judgment can say: *"We should use WebContainers instead of server-side containers because cold-start latency matters more than the long tail of unsupported npm packages for our user profile."* A PM without it defers every architectural decision to engineering and hopes for the best.

| Level | Architecture Judgment Skill |
|-------|---------------------------|
| Foundations | Understand the primitives of modern web execution — browsers, frameworks, build systems, containers, CDNs |
| Level 1 | Compare product architectures across competitors — v0, Lovable, Bolt, Replit Agent — with specific tradeoff reasoning |
| Level 2 | Evaluate runtime and sandbox options; defend a specific choice with cost, latency, and isolation tradeoffs |
| Level 3 | Choose preview and deployment infrastructure; design tenant isolation and rollback mechanisms |
| Level 4 | Assess coding agent architecture; decide between build, integrate, or fork for agent infrastructure |
| Level 5 | Design eval pipeline architecture with appropriate methodologies per quality dimension |
| Level 6 | Instrument the platform with observability matched to its failure modes |
| Level 7 | Own full-stack architectural vision across runtime, preview, deploy, agent, eval, extensibility |

### Abstraction Design

**Defining the right boundaries between platform and product — what to expose, what to hide, what to make configurable.**

Abstraction design is the discipline of drawing lines. Preview environments should be invisible to non-technical builders but inspectable by developers. The tool-use API should let agents do meaningful work without letting them delete the user's project. Where those lines fall is the PM's job.

| Level | Abstraction Design Skill |
|-------|-----------------------|
| Foundations | Recognize where abstraction boundaries live in web platforms |
| Level 1 | Distinguish platform concerns from product-specific concerns |
| Level 2 | Define execution API boundaries — what the runtime exposes vs. isolates |
| Level 3 | Design tenant isolation boundaries and deployment API surfaces |
| Level 4 | Design tool-use contracts for agents — capabilities, parameters, permission model |
| Level 5 | Separate eval concerns into distinct evaluable layers |
| Level 6 | Design observability APIs serving multiple audiences |
| Level 7 | Govern platform-product boundary across multiple consuming teams |

### Failure Reasoning

**Thinking in failure modes — what breaks, who notices, how bad is it, what is the blast radius.**

Every engineering proposal looks good at the happy path. Failure reasoning is the habit of asking: *"What happens if this times out? What if the user's input is malformed? What if a downstream service fails? What is the observability path for each of those?"* Without this habit, platforms ship features that work in demos and break in production.

| Level | Failure Reasoning Skill |
|-------|----------------------|
| Foundations | Recognize common web platform failure patterns |
| Level 1 | Identify failure patterns specific to AI code generation — hallucinations, drift, state corruption |
| Level 2 | Reason about runtime-specific failures — OOM, sandbox escapes, cold start cascades |
| Level 3 | Anticipate preview failure modes — tenant interference, HMR breaks, deploy failures |
| Level 4 | Anticipate agent failure modes — reasoning failures, state corruption, context overflow |
| Level 5 | Anticipate eval system failures — false positives, eval gaming, coverage gaps |
| Level 6 | Design incident response for AI-specific gradual degradation |
| Level 7 | Reason about cascading failures; design blast-radius limits at every boundary |

### Systems Sequencing

**Deciding what to build first when everything depends on everything else, and articulating why.**

Platform roadmaps are dependency graphs. You cannot ship preview infrastructure before the runtime it runs on. You cannot ship evals before the agent they evaluate. You cannot ship extensibility APIs before the underlying API surface is stable. Sequencing judgment is what turns a wishlist into a roadmap.

| Level | Systems Sequencing Skill |
|-------|----------------------|
| Foundations | Understand how web platform primitives build on each other |
| Level 1 | Sequence market entry decisions with competitive context |
| Level 2 | Sequence runtime investments ahead of dependent surfaces |
| Level 3 | Sequence preview, deployment, and observability in dependency order |
| Level 4 | Sequence agent infrastructure, eval system, and production release gates |
| Level 5 | Sequence eval investment before scale, not after |
| Level 6 | Sequence observability to catch the failure modes that matter most at current scale |
| Level 7 | Own multi-quarter platform roadmapping across competing demands |

## How to Use the Competency Map

Think of the four competencies as independent axes of growth. A PM might be strong in Architecture Judgment but weak in Systems Sequencing, or advanced in Failure Reasoning but early in Abstraction Design. Honest self-assessment across all four dimensions helps you focus your learning where it matters most.

As you progress through the modules, pay attention to which competency each exercise targets:

- **Predict prompts** often engage **Failure Reasoning** — they test whether you can anticipate what will break before you read the explanation
- **Calibration Checks** build **Architecture Judgment** — quick tradeoff reasoning against known alternatives
- **Try It Yourself** exercises strengthen **Abstraction Design** — you practice defining where boundaries should fall
- **Reflect prompts** develop **Systems Sequencing** — they ask how you would prioritize or sequence

The framework is diagnostic, not prescriptive. Use it to identify your gaps, then pick the modules that close them fastest.
